title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.
caption:
Death rate from road injuries, 2021

The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.

This choropleth map displays the annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people across the globe for the year 2021, utilizing a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for the lowest rates to dark red for the highest. The legend defines bands starting at 0 and extending to 70 deaths per 100,000, with intermediate thresholds at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60. The data reveals a distinct geographic concentration of high mortality rates in specific regions of Africa and Asia. The highest visible value appears in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, which is shaded in the darkest red band, indicating a rate exceeding 60 deaths per 100,000 people. Surrounding nations in central and western Africa, such as Mali and the Central African Republic, display elevated rates falling within the 30 to 50 range. In Asia, North Korea stands out with a dark red shading comparable to the highest African nations, while neighboring South Korea and Japan show significantly lighter shading in the lowest bands. Conversely, the lowest death rates are visible across western and northern Europe, including countries like Sweden, Norway, and the United Kingdom, as well as Australia and Canada, all of which fall into the lightest color band representing 0 to 10 deaths per 100,000. The United States and Brazil occupy a middle ground with light orange shading, placing them in the 10 to 20 band. Greenland and several other territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no available data. The map shows the highest road injury death rates concentrated in central Africa and the Korean peninsula, while the lowest rates cluster in western Europe and Oceania.
